text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic cholecystectomy, using minimally invasive techniques to remove the gallbladder.
Within the peritoneal cavity, the gallbladder is observed to be completely covered by the omentum.
The surgeon attempts to separate the omentum from the head of the gallbladder, creating space to grasp and retract the gallbladder.
The surgeon gradually separates the gallbladder from surrounding tissues within the peritoneal cavity, using laparoscopic instruments to carefully manipulate the anatomical structures.

The surgeon encounters varying levels of adhesions near the gallbladder, with stronger adhesions present at the fundus. The body of the gallbladder is partially separated from the omentum, and the surgeon is attempting to separate the remaining adhesions on the lateral right side of the patient.
The surgeon observes areas of inflamed omentum, which appear reddish in color, during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon gradually opens up the area around the gallbladder, revealing the possibility of holding the head of the gallbladder with a tooth forceps to push it back.

The surgeon pushes the gallbladder back to expose its base, revealing the attachment to the cystic duct, which connects to the common hepatic duct forming the common bile duct, along with visible blood vessels supplying the area.
The surgeon uses bipolar coagulation on a visible vessel to prevent bleeding, followed by tearing the coagulated vessel apart to ensure hemostasis.
The surgeon uses tooth forceps to grasp the remaining tissue attached to the gallbladder fundus and pushes it back, widening the passages for further dissection.
The surgeon uses tooth forceps to push back the gallbladder fundus, causing some omentum to fall and some to adhere to surrounding structures.

The surgeon dissects within the gallbladder triangle, successfully separating the peritoneal covering from the visceral peritoneum. As the dissection progresses, the distinction between the gallbladder and surrounding vessels becomes visible.

The surgeon works on the left anterior side, where the peritoneum is being carefully peeled off from the gallbladder. The cystic artery is visible, though the tissue is thickened and attached, appearing translucent rather than transparent.

The surgeon uses a tooth grasper to hold a small bile from the gallbladder fundus. Below, the gallbladder appears merged with the common hepatic duct, common bile duct, and portal vein, with visible adhesions. The surgeon employs a Maryland curve dissector to carefully find a cleavage plane, staying close to the gallbladder.

The surgeon carefully hugs the gallbladder while performing gentle dissection to identify and separate the cystic artery from the common hepatic duct and common bile duct, ensuring these ducts are preserved during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the cystic duct leading to the common bile duct below. Above the cystic duct, there is no visible cleavage between the gallbladder and the common hepatic duct. The surgeon attempts to find a plane for cholecystectomy by dissecting above the cystic duct.

The surgeon dissects the gallbladder bed from the hepatic fossa, exposing the liver beneath. The dissection is performed close to the gallbladder to avoid venous sinuses in the liver that may bleed. The cystic artery is identified, appearing stuck to the gallbladder, and is carefully separated despite minor bleeding.

The surgeon carefully isolates the cystic duct at the base of the gallbladder, below the infundibulum, using gentle pressure to separate thickened peritoneal tissue. The dissection progresses slowly due to dense adhesions and inflammation, typical in male patients, with the surgeon maintaining proximity to the gallbladder to avoid damaging the common hepatic duct.

The surgeon carefully enters the space between the gallbladder and surrounding tissues, using a curved Maryland dissector to stay close to the gallbladder. The gallbladder is pulled upwards and towards the left side, revealing the infundibulum and approaching the cystic duct below.

The surgeon carefully peels the gallbladder off from the underlying structures, which include major biliary ducts, the portal vein, and hepatic artery, all encased in fibrous tissue. Using gentle traction and counter-traction, a plane is developed posterior to the gallbladder, ensuring no harm is caused to essential structures.

The surgeon encounters thicker tissue near the gallbladder fundus and proceeds to dissect from the left side or anteriorly. The cystic duct and cystic artery are identified, and a plane is created between them for safe clip application or ligation. The cystic duct is clearly delineated near its base, and the anatomy is well demonstrated with no damage to vital structures.

The surgeon applies clips to the cystic duct and cystic artery, ensuring secure placement with two clips proximally on each structure. The monopolar cautery hook is used to dissect tissue near the fundus of the gallbladder, which appears thicker.

The surgeon observes a single stone within the gallbladder, noting the presence of thick fibrosis indicative of chronic inflammation. The gallbladder is submitted for histopathological examination to rule out malignancy.

The surgeon observes chronic inflammation and adhesions, with visible redness and omentum adherent to the gallbladder. The gallbladder fundus remains slightly attached to the liver, and the surgeon continues dissection while maintaining hemostasis at the gallbladder bed.
